text: Greetings from Sanjay Hospital, Palani.

So today we are presenting a case of a thirty seven year old female who presented to us with complaints of epigastric discomfort and right hypochondrial pain and she was evaluated and found to have symptomatic gallstone disease.
So she was taken up for a laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon begins the procedure by placing standard cholecystectomy ports and enters the abdomen using an open technique at the umbilicus. The peritoneal incision is made anteriorly and extended cranially.
The surgeon dissects the fibrofatty tissue to clearly delineate the Calot's triangle, revealing the anatomical boundaries necessary for further dissection.
The surgeon incises the edge of the peritoneal incision, extending the opening in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection over the fibrofatty tissue to further delineate anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook electrode with coagulating current to cauterize tissue during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon performs a meticulous fiber-by-fiber dissection of the fibrofatty tissue, ensuring hemostasis is maintained throughout the process.
The surgeon observes the transparency of the peritoneal reflection, indicating the thinness and clarity of the tissue layer during the dissection process.
The surgeon makes an incision over the posterior peritoneum, following the previous incision at the edge of the protonal incision.
The surgeon extends the incision superiorly along the posterior peritoneum, continuing the dissection process.
After completing the posterior peritoneal incision, the surgeon returns to the anterior side to continue the procedure.
The surgeon observes the transparency of the peritoneum, indicating a clear view of the underlying structures through the thin membrane.
The surgeon continues to make incisions on the peritoneum, progressing the dissection to further expose underlying structures.
The surgeon has created a peritoneal window, revealing the underlying structures through the transparent peritoneal layer.
The Calot's triangle is now visible, and the surgeon begins dissecting the fibrous fatty tissue within this area to achieve the critical view of safety.
The surgeon attempts to delineate the cystic artery, focusing on its identification and separation from surrounding tissues within the surgical field.
The cystic artery and duct become visible after the previous steps, with the surrounding tissue dissected to enhance their exposure.
The surgeon performs a gradual fiber-by-fiber dissection within the Calot's triangle, carefully separating the fibrous and fatty tissues to achieve the critical view of safety.
The surgeon switches to a Maryland Dissector to skeletonize the cystic duct, preparing it for clip application by carefully dissecting surrounding tissue.
The surgeon strips the peritoneum off the cystic duct, preparing the area for the placement of ligar clips to secure the duct.
The surgeon dissects the fatty tissue surrounding the cystic duct, gradually exposing the duct for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon has nearly achieved the critical view of safety, with the cystic duct and artery clearly visible after meticulous dissection.

The surgeon ensures that only two structures are visible entering the gallbladder, with the Calot's triangle cleared of all fat and fibrous tissue. The lower one-third of the cystic plate is also addressed.
The surgeon uses a Maryland Dissector to continue dissecting the remaining tissue off the cystic duct, ensuring it is fully skeletonized for clip application.
Following the steps of critical review of safety enables us to perform a safe cholecystectomy in order to avoid any biliar vascular injury.
The surgeon carefully dissects the remaining attachments to the cystic duct, removing any residual connective tissue to fully expose the duct.
The surgeon uses a smooth grasper in the left hand to manipulate the gallbladder, aiding in the dissection process.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the remaining fibers surrounding the cystic duct, ensuring it is fully separated from adjacent tissues.
The surgeon switches to an advanced bipolar device, preparing to continue dissection around the cystic duct area.
The surgeon uses an advanced bipolar device to coagulate the cystic artery, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the bipolar cutting function to sever the cystic artery, which has been satisfactorily coagulated.
The surgeon uses a smooth grasper to milk the cystic duct, ensuring there are no remaining stones within the duct.
The surgeon applies a Lager clip to the cystic duct, securing it in preparation for further surgical steps.
The surgeon applies two clips on the cystic duct side and one clip superiorly on the specimen side, securing the duct before further dissection.
The surgeon applies a clip on the specimen side of the cystic duct, ensuring a gap between the previously placed clips on the duct side.
The surgeon introduces a curved scissor into the surgical field, preparing to perform the next step in the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut the cystic duct that has been secured with clips.
The surgeon observes fibrous tissue that may bleed after cutting the lumen and uses a bipolar energy device with the cutting function to address the potential bleeding.
The surgeon switches to a hook instrument, applying traction with the left hand while using coagulation current to make lateral peritoneal incisions, alternating energy with blunt pushing.
The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder from the liver bed, minimizing damage to the gallbladder tissue during the separation process.
The surgeon applies proper traction to assist in dissecting the gallbladder from the liver bed, using a monopolar hook to address fibrous attachments between the gallbladder and liver.
The surgeon encounters dense fibrous attachments while dissecting the gallbladder from the liver bed, using surgical instruments to carefully separate these structures.
So it is important to change the grasp of the left hand instrument.
The camera view is adjusted to provide a closer look at the dissecting area where the gallbladder is being separated from the liver bed.
The surgeon holds the gallbladder near the infundibulum to facilitate dissection from the liver bed, ensuring proximity for effective manipulation.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the gallbladder from the liver bed, with remaining fibrous attachments being carefully separated using a monopolar hook.
The surgeon inspects the cystic duct stem, focusing on its anatomical position and any surrounding tissue attachments.
The surgical field appears clear with no visible bleeding after the dissection near the infundibulum.
The surgeon carefully dissects the final attachments of the gallbladder, ensuring complete hemostasis has been achieved before proceeding.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the gallbladder by addressing the last fibrous attachments and ensuring hemostasis. The gallbladder is then removed through the epigastric port after suctioning and removing bile.
The surgeon removes the intact gallbladder through the epigastric port, completing the cholecystectomy procedure.
Thanks for watching.
